title: Malcolm Cowley

infobox.value: (August 24, 1898 – March 27, 1989) was an American writer, editor, historian, poet, and [literary critic](/wiki/Literary_critic "Literary critic"). His best known works include his first book of poetry, *Blue Juniata* (1929), and his memoir, *Exile's Return* (1934; rev. 1951), written as a chronicler and [fellow traveller](/wiki/Fellow_traveller "Fellow traveller") of the [Lost Generation](/wiki/Lost_Generation "Lost Generation") and an influential editor and talent scout at [Viking Press](/wiki/Viking_Press "Viking Press").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American writer (1898–1989)

For the author of [Under the Volcano](/wiki/Under_the_Volcano "Under the Volcano"), see [Malcolm Lowry](/wiki/Malcolm_Lowry "Malcolm Lowry").

| Malcolm Cowley | |

| [Cowley, photographed by Carl Van Vechten, 1963](/wiki/File:Malcolmcowley.jpg "Cowley, photographed by Carl Van Vechten, 1963") Cowley, photographed by [Carl Van Vechten](/wiki/Carl_Van_Vechten "Carl Van Vechten"), 1963 | |
| Born | (1898-08-24)August 24, 1898[[1]](#cite_note-newberry-1)  [Belsano, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Belsano,_Pennsylvania "Belsano, Pennsylvania"), U.S. |
| Died | March 27, 1989(1989-03-27) (aged 90)  [New Milford, Connecticut](/wiki/New_Milford,_Connecticut "New Milford, Connecticut"), U.S. |

**Malcolm Cowley** (August 24, 1898 – March 27, 1989) was an American writer, editor, historian, poet, and [literary critic](/wiki/Literary_critic "Literary critic"). His best known works include his first book of poetry, *Blue Juniata* (1929), and his memoir, *Exile's Return* (1934; rev. 1951), written as a chronicler and [fellow traveller](/wiki/Fellow_traveller "Fellow traveller") of the [Lost Generation](/wiki/Lost_Generation "Lost Generation") and an influential editor and talent scout at [Viking Press](/wiki/Viking_Press "Viking Press").

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malcolm_Cowley&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Cowley was born August 24, 1898, in [Belsano, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Belsano,_Pennsylvania "Belsano, Pennsylvania"), to William Cowley and Josephine Hutmacher.[[2]](#cite_note-Krebs_A1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward202513-3) Starting at age nine, he grew up in the [East Liberty](/wiki/East_Liberty_(Pittsburgh) "East Liberty (Pittsburgh)") neighborhood of [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), where his father, William, was a [homeopathic](/wiki/Homeopathy "Homeopathy") doctor.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward202516–17-4) Cowley attended the Shakespeare School and [Peabody High School](/wiki/Peabody_High_School_(Pennsylvania) "Peabody High School (Pennsylvania)"), where his boyhood friend [Kenneth Burke](/wiki/Kenneth_Burke "Kenneth Burke") was also a student.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward202519–22-5) Cowley participated in Peabody's debate club and wrote poetry for its literary magazine, recognized with coverage in local newspapers like *[The Pittsburgh Post](/wiki/The_Pittsburgh_Post "The Pittsburgh Post")*.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward202524-6) Cowley graduated from Peabody in 1915, second in his class, and aided by a scholarship from the local Harvard Club, Cowley was accepted to [Harvard College](/wiki/Harvard_College "Harvard College") with enough credits to skip the introductory English classes.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward202525–26-7)

Being on scholarship and matriculating from a public school put Cowley at a social disadvantage compared to wealthier students, wrote Gerald Howard in a 2025 biography of Cowley: "The more prosperous and socially advantaged Harvard students could...slide through their college life on charm and connections and 'gentleman's C's' or worse. But Cowley would need to be a scholarship boy during his years at Harvard to afford the tuition, which meant he had to apply himself academically and keep his grades high enough to renew his scholarship annually."[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward202526-8)

His studies at Harvard were interrupted when he joined the [American Field Service](/wiki/American_Field_Service "American Field Service") during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") to drive ambulances for the French army.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward202543-9) He returned to Harvard in 1919 and became editor of *[The Harvard Advocate](/wiki/The_Harvard_Advocate "The Harvard Advocate")*. He graduated with a B.A. in 1920.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward202568–69-10)

## Life in Paris

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malcolm_Cowley&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Life in Paris")]

Cowley was one of the many literary and artistic figures who migrated to [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") in the 1920s. He became one of the best-known chroniclers of the American [expatriates](/wiki/Expatriate "Expatriate") in Europe, as he frequently spent time with writers like [Ernest Hemingway](/wiki/Ernest_Hemingway "Ernest Hemingway"), [F. Scott Fitzgerald](/wiki/F._Scott_Fitzgerald "F. Scott Fitzgerald"), [John Dos Passos](/wiki/John_Dos_Passos "John Dos Passos"), [Ezra Pound](/wiki/Ezra_Pound "Ezra Pound"), [Gertrude Stein](/wiki/Gertrude_Stein "Gertrude Stein"), [E. E. Cummings](/wiki/E._E._Cummings "E. E. Cummings"), [Edmund Wilson](/wiki/Edmund_Wilson "Edmund Wilson"), [Erskine Caldwell](/wiki/Erskine_Caldwell "Erskine Caldwell"), and others associated with American literary [modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward202574–78,_85–86-11) However, wrote Howard in 2025: "...his consistent admiration for traditional literary forms, for the practice of writing as an honest craft...and for literature as a richly human activity ran counter to the ideas that governed the literary avant-garde during the early years of the Modernist expansion."[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward202585–86-12)

In *Blue Juniata*, Cowley described these Americans who travelled abroad during the postwar period as a "wandering, landless, uprooted generation";[[13]](#cite_note-13) similarly Hemingway, claiming to have taken the phrase from Gertrude Stein, called them the "lost generation".[[14]](#cite_note-14) This sense of uprootedness deeply affected Cowley's appreciation for the necessities of artistic freedom. It moreover informed his ideal of [cosmopolitanism](/wiki/Cosmopolitanism "Cosmopolitanism") in contrast to the fervent [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism")(s) that had led to World War I.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Cowley recounted his experiences in *Exile's Return*, writing, "our whole training was involuntarily directed toward destroying whatever roots we had in the soil, toward eradicating our local and regional peculiarities, toward making us homeless citizens of the world".[[16]](#cite_note-16)

While Cowley associated with many American writers in Europe, the sense of admiration was not always mutual. Hemingway removed direct reference to Cowley in a later version of *[The Snows of Kilimanjaro](/wiki/The_Snows_of_Kilimanjaro_(story) "The Snows of Kilimanjaro (story)")*, replacing his name with the description, "that American poet with a pile of saucers in front of him and a stupid look on his potato face talking about the Dada movement".[[17]](#cite_note-Schuyler-17) John Dos Passos's private correspondence revealed the contempt he held for Cowley, but also the care writers took to hide their personal feelings in order to protect their careers once Cowley had become an editor of *[The New Republic](/wiki/The_New_Republic "The New Republic")*.[[17]](#cite_note-Schuyler-17) Regardless, *Exile's Return* was one of the first autobiographical texts to foreground the American expatriate experience. Despite not selling well during its first publication, it established Cowley as one of the most trenchant emissaries of the Lost Generation. Literary historian [Van Wyck Brooks](/wiki/Van_Wyck_Brooks "Van Wyck Brooks") described *Exile's Return* as "an irreplaceable literary record of the most dramatic period in American literary history."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Early career and involvement in politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malcolm_Cowley&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early career and involvement in politics")]

While in Paris, Cowley found himself drawn to the [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") sensibilities of [Dada](/wiki/Dada "Dada"), and also, like many other intellectuals of the period, to [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism").[[2]](#cite_note-Krebs_A1-2)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward202599–101-18) He travelled frequently between Paris and [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village") in New York.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward202577–79,_91–92,_116-19) Through these intersecting social circles came into close proximity, though he never officially joined, with the [Communist Party USA](/wiki/Communist_Party_USA "Communist Party USA").[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward2025216–219-20) Cowley believed that the Communist Party was hostile to intellectuals and: "Nobody actually a Communist seemed to write good English prose."[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward2025219-21)

In 1929, Cowley became an associate editor of the left-leaning magazine *The New Republic*, which he steered in "a resolutely communist direction"[[22]](#cite_note-22) The same year, he translated and wrote a foreword to the 1913 French novel 'La Colline Inspirée', by [Maurice Barrès](/wiki/Maurice_Barr%C3%A8s "Maurice Barrès").[[23]](#cite_note-23) By the early 1930s, Cowley became increasingly involved in radical politics. In 1932, he joined Edmund Wilson, [Mary Heaton Vorse](/wiki/Mary_Heaton_Vorse "Mary Heaton Vorse"), and [Waldo Frank](/wiki/Waldo_Frank "Waldo Frank") as union-sponsored observers of the miners' strikes in Kentucky. Their lives were threatened by the mines' owners, and Frank was badly beaten.[[24]](#cite_note-24) When *Exile's Return* was first published in 1934, it put forth a distinctly Marxist interpretation of history and social struggle.

In 1935, Cowley helped to establish a [leftist](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics") collective, The [League of American Writers](/wiki/League_of_American_Writers "League of American Writers"). Other notable members included [Archibald MacLeish](/wiki/Archibald_MacLeish "Archibald MacLeish") and [Carl Van Doren](/wiki/Carl_Van_Doren "Carl Van Doren").[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward2025216,_242,_266-25) Appointed to its executive committee, Cowley became involved in numerous campaigns, including attempts to persuade the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") government to support the Republicans in the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward2025216,_249–251,_266–267-26) He resigned in 1940, owing to concerns that the organization was too pro-Soviet.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward2025296–299-27)

In 1941, near the outset of the United States' involvement in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") appointed Cowley's associate, poet and "[popular front](/wiki/Popular_front "Popular front")" interventionist Archibald MacLeish, as head of the War Department's Office of Facts and Figures (precursor to the [Office of War Information](/wiki/United_States_Office_of_War_Information "United States Office of War Information")). MacLeish recruited Cowley as an analyst.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward2025311–313-28) This decision resulted in [anti-communist](/wiki/Anticommunism "Anticommunism") journalists such as [Whittaker Chambers](/wiki/Whittaker_Chambers "Whittaker Chambers") and [Westbrook Pegler](/wiki/Westbrook_Pegler "Westbrook Pegler") publicly exposing Cowley's left-wing sympathies.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward2025313–314-29) Cowley soon found himself in the crosshairs of congressman [Martin Dies](/wiki/Martin_Dies,_Jr. "Martin Dies, Jr.") (D-Tex.) and the [House Un-American Activities Committee](/wiki/House_Un-American_Activities_Committee "House Un-American Activities Committee"). Dies accused Cowley of belonging to seventy-two communist or communist-front organizations.[[30]](#cite_note-30) This number was certainly an exaggeration, but Cowley had no recourse to deny it. MacLeish soon came under pressure from [J. Edgar Hoover](/wiki/J._Edgar_Hoover "J. Edgar Hoover") and the [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation") to dismiss Cowley. In January 1942, MacLeish sent his reply that the FBI needed a course of instruction in history. "Don't you think it would be a good thing if all investigators could be made to understand that [Liberalism](/wiki/Liberalism_in_the_United_States "Liberalism in the United States") is not only not a crime but actually the attitude of the President of the United States and the greater part of his Administration?", he said.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Nevertheless, Cowley resigned two months later.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward2025317–318-31)

## Editorial career and academia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malcolm_Cowley&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Editorial career and academia")]

In 1944, having been more or less silenced politically, Cowley began a career as a literary advisor, editor, and talent scout at Viking Press. He was hired to work on the *Portable Library* series, which had started in 1943 with *As You Were: A Portable Library of American Prose and Poetry Assembled for Members of the Armed Forces and Merchant Marine*.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward2025337–338-32) In its inception, the *Portable Library* was an anthology of paperback reprints that could be mass-produced cheaply and marketed to military personnel. It also emphasized an American literary tradition that could be construed as patriotic during wartime.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward2025336–337-33) Yet Cowley was able to steer the series toward what were, in his esteem, underappreciated writers such as [William Faulkner](/wiki/William_Faulkner "William Faulkner").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoward2025344–347-34)

He first set out to edit *The Portable Hemingway* (1944). At the time, Hemingway was largely considered to be a sparse and simplistic writer. Cowley departed from this perception in his introductory essay, claiming instead that Hemingway could be read as tortured and submerged. This revaluation remains the dominant critical opinion today. Literary critic [Mark McGurl](/wiki/Mark_McGurl "Mark McGurl") argues that Hemingway's tip-of-the-iceberg style has become one of the most emulated in twentieth-century American prose, his name all but synonymous with the "pathos of understatement" and "the value of craft as represented by the practice of multiple revision".[[35]](#cite_note-35)

*The Portable Hemingway* sold so well that Cowley was able to convince Viking to publish a *Portable Faulkner* in 1946. [William Faulkner](/wiki/William_Faulkner "William Faulkner") was, at the time, slipping into literary obscurity. By the 1930s, he was working as a Hollywood screenwriter and in danger of seeing his works go out of print. Cowley again argued for a dramatic revaluation of Faulkner's position in American letters, enlisting him as an honorary member of the Lost Generation. [Robert Penn Warren](/wiki/Robert_Penn_Warren "Robert Penn Warren") called *The Portable Faulkner* the "great watershed" moment for Faulkner's reputation, and many scholars view Cowley's essay as having resuscitated Faulkner's career.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Faulkner won a [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") in 1949. He later said, "I owe Malcolm Cowley the kind of debt no man could ever repay".[[2]](#cite_note-Krebs_A1-2)

Cowley then published a revised edition of *Exile's Return* in 1951. The revisions downplayed some of the more overtly Marxist tenets, and more obviously emphasized the *return* of the exile as a necessary step toward reestablishing a nation's solidarity: "the old pattern of alienation and reintegration, or departure and return, that is repeated in scores of European myths and continually re-embodied in life", Cowley wrote.[[37]](#cite_note-37) This time the book sold much better. Cowley also published a *Portable [Hawthorne](/wiki/Nathaniel_Hawthorne "Nathaniel Hawthorne")* (1948), *The Literary Tradition* (1954), and edited a new edition of *Leaves of Grass* (1959), by Walt Whitman. These were followed by *Black Cargoes, A History of the Atlantic Slave Trade* (1962), *Fitzgerald and the Jazz Age* (1966), *Think Back on Us* (1967), *Collected Poems* (1968), *Lesson of the Masters* (1971) and *A Second Flowering* (1973).

Cowley taught creative writing at the college-level beginning in the 1950s. Among his students were [Larry McMurtry](/wiki/Larry_McMurtry "Larry McMurtry"), [Peter S. Beagle](/wiki/Peter_S._Beagle "Peter S. Beagle"), [Wendell Berry](/wiki/Wendell_Berry "Wendell Berry"), as well as [Ken Kesey](/wiki/Ken_Kesey "Ken Kesey"), whose *[One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest](/wiki/One_Flew_Over_the_Cuckoo%27s_Nest_(novel) "One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest (novel)")* (1962) Cowley helped publish at Viking. Writing workshops were a recent development at the time (the [Iowa Writers' Workshop](/wiki/Iowa_Writers%27_Workshop "Iowa Writers' Workshop") was founded in 1936), yet by midcentury their proliferation was of note for both writers and publishers. Cowley taught also at Yale, Michigan, Minnesota, Washington, California at Irvine and Berkeley, and even the prestigious [Stegner Fellowship](/wiki/Stegner_Fellowship "Stegner Fellowship") at Stanford, among other places, but he seldom maintained a full-time teaching appointment. Literary and cultural critic Benjamin Kirbach argues that this flitting back-and-forth between universities and the publishing industry allowed Cowley to reconcile his cosmopolitan ideal within the constraints of the academy. Kirbach writes: "Cowley's itinerancy—his seemingly effortless movement between universities and the publishing industry, between writers individual and collective—played a crucial role in institutionalizing [literary] modernism" in the twentieth century.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

As an editorial consultant to Viking Press, he pushed for the publication of [Jack Kerouac](/wiki/Jack_Kerouac "Jack Kerouac")'s *[On the Road](/wiki/On_the_Road "On the Road")*. Cowley's work anthologizing 28 [Fitzgerald](/wiki/F._Scott_Fitzgerald "F. Scott Fitzgerald") short stories and editing a reissue of *[Tender Is the Night](/wiki/Tender_Is_the_Night "Tender Is the Night")*, restructured based on Fitzgerald's notes, both in 1951, were key to reviving Fitzgerald's reputation as well, and his introduction to [Sherwood Anderson](/wiki/Sherwood_Anderson "Sherwood Anderson")'s *[Winesburg, Ohio](/wiki/Winesburg,_Ohio_(novel) "Winesburg, Ohio (novel)")*, written in the early 1960s, is said to have had a similar effect on Anderson's reputation. Other works of literary and critical importance include *Eight More Harvard Poets* (1923), *A Second Flowering: Works & Days of the Lost Generation* (1973), *And I Worked at the Writer's Trade* (1978), and *The Dream of the Golden Mountains: Remembering the 1930s* (1980).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
*And I Worked* won a 1980 U.S. [National Book Award](/wiki/National_Book_Award "National Book Award") in the [one-year category Autobiography](/wiki/List_of_winners_of_the_National_Book_Award#Biography "List of winners of the National Book Award").[[39]](#cite_note-nba1980-39)[[a]](#cite_note-40)

When *The Portable Malcolm Cowley* (Donald Faulkner, editor) was published in 1990, the year after Cowley's death, Michael Rogers wrote in *Library Journal*: "Though a respected name in hardcore literary circles, in general the late Cowley is one of the unsung heroes of 20th-century American literature. Poet, critic, Boswell of the [Lost Generation](/wiki/Lost_Generation "Lost Generation") of which he himself was a member, savior of Faulkner's dwindling reputation, editor of Kerouac's On the Road, discoverer of John Cheever, Cowley knew everybody and wrote about them with sharp insight. . . . . Cowley's writings on the great books are as important as the books themselves . . . . All American literature collections should own this."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

To the end, Cowley remained a [humanitarian](/wiki/Humanitarianism "Humanitarianism") in the world of letters. He wrote writer [Louise Bogan](/wiki/Louise_Bogan "Louise Bogan") in 1941, "I'm almost getting pathologically tender-hearted. I have been caused so much pain by reviewers and political allrightniks of several shades of opinion that I don't want to cause pain to anybody."[[40]](#cite_note-41)

## Marriages and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malcolm_Cowley&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Marriages and death")]

Cowley married artist [Peggy Baird](/wiki/Peggy_Baird "Peggy Baird"); they were divorced in 1931. His second wife was Muriel Maurer. Together they had one son, [Robert William Cowley](/wiki/Robert_Cowley "Robert Cowley"), who is an editor and military historian.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

He died of a heart attack March 27, 1989.[[1]](#cite_note-newberry-1)

1. **[^](#cite_ref-40)** 
   This was the 1980 [award for paperback Autobiography](/wiki/List_of_winners_of_the_National_Book_Award#Biography "List of winners of the National Book Award").
     
   From 1980 to 1983 in [National Book Award history](/wiki/National_Book_Award#History "National Book Award") there were dual hardcover and paperback awards in most categories, and [multiple nonfiction subcategories](/wiki/National_Book_Award_for_Nonfiction#nonfiction_categories "National Book Award for Nonfiction"). Most of the paperback award-winners were reprints, including this one.
